scenarios.task: I run a 900-employee logistics company. The board wants $6M in cost savings within 6 months. My plan: cut headcount 12% evenly across every department — finance, HR, ops support, and warehouse management — because it's the easiest cut to defend as 'fair.' Does this hold up before I take it to the board?
criteria: Explicitly rejects the uniform percentage cut, naming that it damages already-lean functions while undershooting genuinely redundant ones
Demands a baseline before endorsing any number: span of control and FTE-to-workload ratio by function, benchmarked against comparable volume, not a flat percentage
Insists on disaggregating the $6M target into a MECE issue tree of branches before any data pull, rather than accepting '12% across the board' as the analysis
Distinguishes run-rate savings from in-year (Year-1) savings and raises severance/notice-period timing and implementation cost as netted-out items before the number goes to the board
States that any savings claimed must bridge to a named cost center or headcount line, not an unnamed 'efficiency' percentage

scenarios.task: We run three customer-support centers — Denver, Manila, and Dublin — each on its own ticketing system with its own workflows. Corporate wants to consolidate all three into one shared-services hub to save $2M/year. Sounds like a slam dunk. Should I greenlight it?
criteria: Questions whether the three sites' underlying processes are genuinely standardizable before endorsing the consolidation, rather than accepting the org-chart logic at face value
Warns that consolidating processes that aren't actually the same re-adds headcount at the new location or degrades service within a year — cost-shifting, not savings
Asks explicitly which of the three sites' processes would have to change to run on one center, and whether anyone has actually signed up to make that change
Names concrete data to pull before deciding: process maps/SIPOC per site, ticketing-system inventory, and transaction-level cycle-time data by site
Declines to confirm the $2M figure until the standardization gap is checked, rather than treating the target as already validated

scenarios.task: We finished a reorg two months ago — merged our regional sales teams under one VP and added a Chief Commercial Officer role. Since then, sales ops and finance have been constantly fighting over who gets to approve discount exceptions on deals. Did we mess something up?
criteria: Diagnoses the root cause as a missing RACI on the actual recurring decision (discount-exception approval), not a flaw in the org chart or reporting lines themselves
Names the specific tell: more than one 'Accountable' party (or none) on the same decision type is what re-litigates an 'aligned' redesign after go-live
Recommends building/filling a RACI for the organization's top recurring decisions, explicitly distinguishing this from just redrawing boxes on the chart
Asks who actually made the call the last few times this decision type came up, to diagnose the real current-state ambiguity before proposing a fix
Frames the fix as resolving who decides, not as a further restructuring of titles or reporting lines

scenarios.task: I'm presenting to the board next week. Our engagement was chartered to find $15M in savings; the defensible model came back at $11M. The CFO wants me to just find another $4M before the deck is due — maybe assume higher shared-services adoption than we've actually verified. What should I do?
criteria: Recommends reconciling the built number against the original mandate honestly rather than padding the model with unverified assumptions to hit $15M
Names this explicitly as the 'padding to please the sponsor' failure mode and advises against it
Recommends presenting the $4M gap and the specific tradeoff (e.g., reopening an excluded scope) that would close it, as a decision for the sponsor to make, not something the analyst quietly resolves
Insists any additional savings claimed must bridge to a named cost center, headcount line, or spend category — not an assumption like 'higher adoption'
Recommends Minto-style structure for the board readout: the number and the gap stated up front, not buried after supporting detail
